---
title: "Project 3"
subtitle: "STATS 220 Semester One 2026"
author: "Aren Jade Madres"
output:
  html_document:
    code_folding: hide
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
selected_photos <- read_csv("selected_photos.csv")
```

## Introduction
The two words I used to search for my photos on pexels.com was **Indonesia** and **temple**, the reason why I chose these words was because at beginning of 2026, I went to Indonesia for a month to spend time with my long distance girlfriend, we went to see a lot of temples, ever since I've come back to New Zealand, I've been missing her so I made my project 3 revolving around Indonesian Temples.

[My Search for Indonesia Temples](https://www.pexels.com/search/indonesia%20temple/)

![First row of search results](top_photos.png)

The photo's I typically see are mostly portrait, probably to capture the sheer height these temples are, most of the photos I inspected had a 1-5 likes, but occasionally there would be a photo of a temple with around 40-50 likes. Most colours I see in these photos are grey, mostly highlighting the temples themselves, with the occasional green from the grass and it's landscape, what I found most interesting is that the saturation is turned all the way down for most of the photos of the temples, probably wanting to theme the colour scheme around the temple itself.

```{r}
selected_photos %>%
  select(url) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

## Key features of my selected photos

```{r}
mean_aspect_ratio <- selected_photos$aspect_ratio %>% mean(na.rm = TRUE)
mean_megapixels <- selected_photos$megapixels %>% mean(na.rm = TRUE)
landscape_count <- sum(selected_photos$classification == "landscape")
max_megapixels <- selected_photos$megapixels %>% max(na.rm = TRUE)
```
They key features of the photos selected are the number of landscape photos, the average aspect ratio of these photos, the average megapixels and the highest megapixel count. 

The number of selected photos under the classification of landscape is ,  `r landscape_count`, meaning, with my calculations, these would be landscape where the photo is wider than it is tall.

The selected photos have an average aspect ratio of `r round(mean_aspect_ratio, 2)`, this means that of the selected photos, they are landscape, which is weird since I believed most of the images were portrait, this could be with how the website shows their images. 

With an average megapixel of `r round(mean_megapixels, 2)` we can assume that the selected photos have high resolution, which makes sense as the photos on the website look high quality. 

The highest megapixels we can see is `r round(max_megapixels, 2)`, this shows a very high quality of a photo, which means that Pexels API is capable of providing high resolution photos.

## Creativity
![](creativity.gif)

In this project, I made an animated GIF using 3 photos from my selected photos data frame. Most of the skills I used was from Module 1, specifically for gif-making, the information I used to make the gifs came from the labs and the first initial project, which I expanded on that skill with using more than one photo for my gifs, alongside with giving the photos I used a nice tint that highlights their image nicely. I think this was a good use of creativity because I'm not making my images too convoluted with a bunch of effects, rather I'm highlighting the already nice photos with a nice tone. My exploration.R used a lot of the skills that I gained from module 2, with using the $ to access columns from data frames which allowed me to use functions such as sum and inbuilt mathematical operators that was taugh in those previous modules. 

## Learning reflection

This module was pretty heavy when it came to built in functions and just overall content, I think this is because I'm learning a lot new stuff here such as working with API's, most of the inbuilt function act similar to the once you see in computer science like case_when but if I were to say the most important thing in this module was how to source data from an API. I had no clue that websites like Pexels could allow me to request data from a website to a IDEs, and with using parameters like per_page = 80, I could access 80 photos worth of data. This made me want to work more with API's and source data from them so I could work with them in the future, it is pretty interesting on how much data you can obtain through API's. 


## Appendix

```{r file='exploration.R', eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}

```
```{r file='project3_report.Rmd', eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}

```
